text: The surgeon places a Lone Star retractor to expose the surgical field and creates a purse-string suture to close the lumen of the rectum above the dentate line, ensuring it is below the level of the tumor.
The surgeon uses cautery to make a full-thickness incision in the anal canal, extending approximately two centimeters cephalad beyond the rectal wall above the level of the sphincters.
The surgeon inserts the TAMUS platform into the rectal cavity and connects the air seal, preparing the surgical field for further intervention.

The surgeon identifies the mesorectal plane and reworks it circumferentially, progressing towards the top of the rectum. The posterior mesorectum is visualized, followed by lateral and anterior dissection on both sides.
The surgeon halts the perineal dissection at the anterior level of the peritoneal reflection, marking the boundary of the dissection area.
The surgeon gains access to the abdominal cavity using an open Hassan technique, inserting laparoscopic ports into the abdomen.
The surgeon creates an incision at the root of the mesentery of the sigmoid colon near the promontory, then moves cephalad towards the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA).
The surgeon works medially to laterally, carefully preserving the retroperitoneal structures. The takeoff of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is skeletonized and divided between clips.
The surgeon continues retroperitoneal dissection cephalad towards the splenic flexure, using a harmonic scalpel to dissect the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV).

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take down the white line of Toldt at the lateral aspect of the descending colon, then approaches the transverse colon and enters the lesser sac to complete the mobilization of the splenic flexure.

The surgical team focuses on the pelvis, using cautery to dissect the retro-rectal space. They proceed laterally on both sides of the rectum, eventually moving anteriorly to connect with the transanal total mesorectal excision (TATME) portion.
The surgeon completes the circumferential dissection around the rectum, joining the previously dissected areas in the retro-rectal space.
The surgeon removes the detached specimen from the pelvis into the abdominal cavity. The pelvis is then irrigated with saline and bacitracin solution.

The surgeon examines the large bowel, noting a bluish discoloration with a clear demarcation on the distal bowel. The left branch of the middle colic artery is visible, held by a grasper on the left side.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to further mobilize the transverse colon, continuing the dissection and separation of tissue planes.

The surgeon performs a retro ileal colorectal anastomosis, passing the transverse colon behind the distal lower arcades at the ileocolic junction. A window is bluntly created at the mesentery level, carefully avoiding all visible vessels.
The surgeon verifies the orientation of the large bowel, ensuring it has adequate mobility. The small bowel is checked to confirm it is not trapped underneath the large bowel.

The surgical team removes the TAMIS platform and delivers the rectal stump through the anus. The sigmoid, descending, and transverse colon are then pulled through, revealing an area with good vascularization.
The surgeon divides the bowel, ensuring hemostasis, and proceeds to create an end-to-end anastomosis using a hand-sewn technique.
The surgeon matures a diverting ileostomy, creating a visible stoma on the abdominal surface.
